solutions.solution: Check for complementary pair: (a) Check if any two given conclusions have the same subject and same predicate. (b) If yes, then check that none of them has been marked as a valid conclusion after step II or as a case of immediate inference. (c) If none of them has been marked as a valid conclusion, then form a complementary pair if they are an A-O or an I-E pair. (d) If they do make a complementary pair then mark the one that follows.

A summary of the method: The entire process of solving three-statement syllogism is performed in three simple steps. These three steps have been already described above. To make it clearer, we are giving below the gist of these three steps:

Perform step I. In other words, find out the relevant statements for testing a given conclusion.

Perform step II. In other words, use the formulae to test a given conclusion. If more than two statements are relevant for a given conclusion, use the chain-like formulae. If the given conclusion has been rejected in step II,
Perform step III: (i). In other words, check for immediate inference.
If the given conclusion has still not been accepted
Perform step III: (ii). In other words, check for complementary pair.
(Note: You need to perform Step III (i), if a conclusion has already been accepted in step II. Again, you do not need to perform step III (i), if a given conclusion has already been accepted in step III (i).)

Complete step-by-step solution of Ex 1: We hope that the method has become clear to you by now. If there are any doubts, see the detailed solution of Ex 1 that is given below. We have already given the details of step I for Ex 1. So, this solution will start from step II onwards.

Conclusion I: We have seen that for this conclusion, statements A and B are relevant. Now we perform step II. For this, we first arrange A and B in such a way that the predicate of the first sentence is the subject of the second. This is done as: Some cars are docks. (I)

All docks are boxes (A) Now, we have, I + A = I. So, the conclusion will be of type I and it will be 'Some cars are boxes.' Hence, conclusion I is valid. (Now, we do not need to perform step III because the conclusion has been accepted in step II itself.)

Conclusion II: We have seen that statements A and C are relevant for this conclusion (step I). Now, we perform step II. For this, we write the sentences in such a way that the predicate of the first sentence is the subject of the second. This is done as: All docks are boxes. (A)

No boxes are chocolates. (E) Now, we have A + E = E. Hence, the conclusion will be 'No docks are chocolates'. Now, the given conclusion is: 'No cars are chocolates'. However, since 'No docks are chocolates' automatically implies 'No chocolates are docks', Hence, this conclusion is also valid. (Again we do not need to perform step III because the conclusion has been accepted in step II itself.)

Conclusion III: We have seen that all the three statements are relevant for this conclusion (step I). So, we should perform step II. For this we should write the three statements in such a way that the predicate of the first sentence is the subject of the second and the predicate of the second sentence is the subject of the first. This is done as: Some cars are docks. (I)

All docks are boxes. (A) Now, we have the chain-like formula: I + A + E = I + E = O. Thus, a valid conclusion should be of type O, that is 'Some cars are not chocolates'. But this is conclusion III. Hence, conclusion III is valid. But conclusion III is not accepted in our case. So, we perform step III.(i): We see that 'Some cars are not chocolates' does not follow from any definitive given statements above. So, this test fails. Now, we perform step III.(ii): We will have to first search any other conclusion that has the same subject and predicate. We see that conclusion IV fully matches with conclusion III and the predicate of conclusions III and IV are the same: 'cars' and 'chocolates' respectively. Again, conclusion III is of type I and conclusion IV is of type O and we know that an I-O pair makes a complementary pair.

Still, we would not choose the choice 'either of them follows' unless we are sure that step III and step III (i) fail on conclusion IV too. But we see that conclusion IV is not accepted in step II. So, despite the fact that conclusion III and IV make a complementary pair, we do not accept the choice 'either of them follows' because conclusion IV is accepted in step II.

Conclusion IV: As already explained, it is a valid conclusion.
Answer: 4 (I, II and IV follow).
